retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Sodium Channels -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Due to the extensive distribution of sodium channels in a wide array of organ systems, care is necessary when prescribing or administering sodium channel blockers. Lidocaine, for instance, is a potent local anesthetic used routinely for procedures and laceration repairs but does carry the risk for systemic release. High doses of lidocaine released into systemic circulation can be toxic to both the CNS and cardiovascular system causing seizures, AV heart block, arrhythmias, and more. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Bupivacaine -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Conduction of nerve impulses is through the generation of an action potential along an axon — local anesthesia results when LAs bind the Na+ channel and inhibit the Na+ permeability necessary for the action potential. Local anesthetics selectively inhibit the open form of voltage-gated Na+ channels. Na+ channel blockade results in the decrease or elimination of conduction in vascular smooth muscle, leading to relaxation. In the heart, this leads to decreased pacemaker activity and prolongation of the refractory period. This action is unique to bupivacaine due to its decreased rate of dissociation from blocked sodium channels, which leads to prolongation of the maximal rate of depolarization (Vmax) and the potential for ventricular arrhythmias. Also, LAs produce a dose-dependent myocardial depression and interference with Ca2+ signaling within the cardiac muscle because they also bind and inhibit cardiac voltage-gated Ca2+ and K+ channels.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Sodium Channels -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Voltage-gated sodium channels are the target of multiple pharmacological compounds, including anesthetics like lidocaine, antiepileptics like phenytoin and lamotrigine, and antiarrhythmics like flecainide and mexiletine. The prevention of nerve conduction is the mechanism of drugs like lidocaine blocks the potentiation of pain signals, while antiepileptics such as phenytoin and lamotrigine lower the seizure threshold by similarly reducing neuron excitability. [10] In all instances, the object is to reduce the influx of sodium to prevent the generation of repeated action potentials by stabilizing the membrane potential. The prevention of action potentials, particularly on the presynaptic neuron, prevents downstream signal transmission by inhibiting the release of neurotransmitters at the synaptic cleft.

retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Sodium Channels -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: The structure of voltage-gated sodium channels consists of one pore-forming alpha subunit through which the sodium will pass and one to two beta subunits.

retrieved_docs.title: Antiarrhythmic Medications -- Mechanism of Action -- Class IV Antiarrhythmics
retrieved_docs.content: L-type Ca 2+ current blockers (eg, verapamil, diltiazem): Blockage of Ca 2+ current, resulting in inhibition of SAN pacing, AVN conduction, and suppression of intracellular Ca 2+ signaling; increased PR intervals. Useful for rate control in atrial fibrillation. [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Calcium Channel Blockers -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: CCBs block the inward movement of calcium by binding to the L-type “long-acting” voltage-gated calcium channels in the heart, vascular smooth muscle, and pancreas. Two major categories of CCBs are based on their primary physiological effects. The non-dihydropyridines exert inhibitory effects on the SA and AV nodes, thereby slowing cardiac conduction and contractility. This property allows for the treatment of hypertension, reduces oxygen demand, and helps to control the rate of tachydysrhythmias.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Sodium Channels -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Voltage-gated sodium channels have two gates: an activating gate that is voltage-dependent and an inactivating gate that is time-dependent. The opening of the activating gate allows the influx of sodium and cell depolarization. The closing of the inactivation gate will stop the flow of sodium regardless of the status of the activation gate. These two gates work in tandem to ensure that depolarization occurs in a controlled manner: after being open for a few milliseconds, the voltage-gated sodium channels will inactivate, stopping the flow of sodium, even in the presence of persistent stimulation. The channel will remain unable to open again until the cell repolarizes to a threshold voltage that varies depending on the cell type. The clinical implication is that in situations of sustained depolarization, the voltage-gated sodium channel will stop working, preventing the cells from becoming more and more positive. This mechanism is an important safeguard to ensure that unimpeded depolarization cannot occur. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Local Anesthetics in Children -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Local anesthetics are primarily used to reversibly block action potentials that create impulse conduction along neural axons carrying sensory or motor signals. This occurs by blocking voltage-gated sodium channels. [8] Acting as weak bases in their ionized form, LAs block nerve conduction at the Na+ channel when it is inactive, thus preventing the opening and flux of ions. [3] Isolated LAs can inhibit platelet aggregation and the production of free radicals, therefore producing anti-inflammatory properties. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Lidocaine Toxicity(Archived) -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Most local anesthetics block voltage-gated sodium channels within the cell, preventing subsequent channel activation and interfering with the large transient sodium influx that occurs during membrane depolarization. Impulse conduction slows, the rate of rising and the magnitude of the action potential decrease, and the threshold for excitation is raised progressively until an action potential can no longer be generated and impulse propagation is abolished. Hence, the pronounced effect of lidocaine toxicity on cells relies on the propagation of action potentials, such as those in the central nervous and myocardial conduction systems. Local anesthetics may also block calcium and potassium channels, as well as N-methyl-d-aspartate (NMDA) receptors, to varying degrees.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Sodium Channels -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Alpha subunits: The alpha subunits are made up of four transmembrane domains, with each domain consisting of six segments. The voltage sensory component is on the fourth segment of each alpha domain. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Sodium Channels -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Beta subunits: The genes SCN1B–SCN4B encode five identified beta subunits . Interestingly, the beta subunits are actually a part of the Ig superfamily of cell adhesion molecules due to their large, extracellular V-set immunoglobulin (Ig) domain. [4] [5] While the beta subunit does not have a sodium channel pore, it is important in regulating excitability by modulating the localization, gating, and kinetics of the pore on the alpha subunit adjacent to it.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Sodium Channels -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: To perform their functions, voltage-gated sodium channels must be targeted to specific cellular domains and interact with multiple membrane, extracellular matrix, and cytoskeletal proteins, forming multiprotein complexes. Mutations in different proteins of the complex can result in similar clinical phenotypes because the integrity of the whole complex is fundamental for the function of the voltage-gated sodium channels.

retrieved_docs.title: Mexiletine -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Mexiletine is a sodium channel blocker and further classified as a Class 1B antiarrhythmic in the Vaughan-Williams classification scheme of antiarrhythmic drugs. Mexiletine is an oral medication that blocks sodium channels in cardiac myocytes and nerve cells. In cardiac myocytes, mexiletine affects phase 0 of the cardiac myocyte action potential, inhibiting the inward sodium current, and its mechanism of action can be generalized across all Class 1B antiarrhythmics. [2] The uniqueness of this subclass of sodium channel blockers is because of the type of sodium channels they best bind to and their effect on the cardiac action potential with relative changes on EKG. To further understand the properties of the Class 1B drugs, it is essential to review the cardiac myocyte action potential. The action potential divides into five separate phases, phases 0 to 4 (Figure 1), where the sodium channels are responsible for the depolarization from the negative, approximately -85 to -90 mV, resting membrane potential to a positive depolarized state, termed phase 0 of the cardiac action potential. The class 1B antiarrhythmics bind best to sodium channels in the depolarized state, which correlates with the primary use of class 1B antiarrhythmics and their value in ischemic arrhythmias. [11] Ischemia causes damage to the cardiac myocytes, rendering them unable to maintain their negative resting membrane potential, producing an increase in the number of cardiac myocytes in the depolarized state. Moreover, the class 1B drugs have the least effect on the cardiac action potential compared to the other class 1 drugs, likely secondary to their binding specificity.

retrieved_docs.title: Sodium Channel Blocker Toxicity -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Hypertonic saline is considered in refractory cases when patients remain unstable after optimizing fluids, vasopressors, and sodium bicarbonate (ie, the patient's pH is 7.55). [15] This remains an option in dire circumstances, as reported in a case of flecainide overdose. [16] Lidocaine, a class Ib antidysrhythmic, is also used for refractory dysrhythmia due to sodium channel blockade. Lidocaine has favorable kinetics, with fast on and fast off binding of sodium channels, competing for binding sites. The result is increased periods with no drug bound, leading to restoration of sodium channel function.

retrieved_docs.title: Mexiletine -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Lei et al discuss a modern classification of antiarrhythmic drugs, incorporating advances that have been made since the original Vaughan Williams classification was introduced in 1970. [14] It incorporates findings related to Nav1.5 which is preferentially expressed in atrial, Purkinje conducting and ventricular as opposed to SAN and AVN cardiomyocyte. Class Ib drugs bind preferentially to the Nav1.5 inactivated state from which they dissociate relatively rapidly making class Ib drugs particularly effective in reducing ventricular arrhythmias due to Nav 1.5 channels remaining inactive for the longest duration in ventricular tissue. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1526
retrieved_docs.content: Antiarrhythmics, class Ib are a group of sodium‑channel–blocking agents that preferentially affect depolarized cardiac tissue, and mexiletin is a member of this class. As a class Ib antiarrhythmic, mexiletin is employed in the management of ventricular arrhythmias and other rhythm disturbances, reflecting the pharmacologic properties shared by drugs within the Antiarrhythmics, class Ib category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 1718
retrieved_docs.content: Antiarrhythmic drugs represent a diverse pharmacologic group used to treat cardiac rhythm disturbances. Among them, cardiac glycosides are classified as antiarrhythmics because they modulate myocardial contractility and conduction. Nizofenone, although primarily known as a neuroprotective compound, also exhibits antiarrhythmic activity and is considered part of the antiarrhythmic category. Prokainamide hydrochloride is a well‑established class IA antiarrhythmic agent that blocks sodium channels and prolongs the action potential. Together, these agents illustrate the broad spectrum of mechanisms encompassed by antiarrhythmic therapy, highlighting the importance of selecting appropriate agents based on their specific pharmacologic properties for optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 90
retrieved_docs.content: delineate a network of adrenergic antagonists, peripheral α‑blockers, and antiarrhythmic agents, their hierarchical classifications, receptor targets, chemical taxonomy, physiologic effects, and clinically relevant contraindications, providing clinicians with an integrated framework for therapeutic selection and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2476
retrieved_docs.content: Antiarrhythmics, class Ia is a pharmacologic subclass of antiarrhythmic agents that encompasses the drugs ajmalin and prajmalin. Both ajmalin and prajmalin belong to this class, sharing the characteristic sodium‑channel blocking mechanism and similar clinical applications in the management of ventricular and supraventricular arrhythmias. Recognizing that Antiarrhythmics, class Ia includes both ajmalin and prajmalin clarifies its classification and supports appropriate therapeutic decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 2 -- Community 944
retrieved_docs.content: be used to treat تسرع القلب الانتيابي, but it is contraindicated in إِحْصار القلب غير محدد, indicating that while adenosine can terminate certain tachycardias, it should be avoided in patients with unspecified heart block due to the risk of exacerbating conduction failure. Together, these relationships delineate a network in which various rhythm disorders, their anatomical substrates, therapeutic options, and contraindications are interlinked, providing a comprehensive framework for understanding and managing cardiac conduction and rhythm abnormalities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 1 -- Community 256
retrieved_docs.content: acting anti‑adrenergic class. Understanding these pharmacologic classes, their mechanisms (ACE inhibition, AT₁ blockade, renin inhibition, calcium‑channel blockade, α₁ antagonism, central sympatholysis, potassium‑channel activation) and their combinatorial use enables clinicians to tailor antihypertensive therapy to individual patient characteristics and optimize cardiovascular risk management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 1 -- Community 200
retrieved_docs.content: unspecified heart block. Diagnosis relies on heart‑rate measurement, electrocardiographic monitoring, and cardiac electrophysiology studies that incorporate arrhythmia induction, electrophysiologic evaluation, and ablation of arrhythmogenic foci for conditions such as atrial fibrillation, supraventricular tachycardia, and ventricular tachycardia; the atrioventricular node is a common anatomic locus for many of these blocks and for paroxysmal tachycardia. Therapeutically, antidromic tachycardia may be treated with adenosine monophosphate (contra‑indicated in unspecified heart block), while supraventricular tachycardia and related disorders respond to the calcium‑channel blocker verapamil; meclizine hydrochloride is used to alleviate dizziness and prevent vomiting associated with conduction abnormalities. Conduction disorders are also linked to autonomic nervous system dysfunction and may coexist with congestive cardiomyopathy, which itself is clinically associated with left‑ventricular

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 2 -- Community 579
retrieved_docs.content: induction, arrhythmogenic focus ablation, and treatment of ventricular tachycardia target the intrinsic accelerated ventricular rhythm (نظْمٌ بُطَينِيٌّ ذاتِيٌّ مُعَجَّل) and its subtype ventricular acceleration (التسارع البطيني). Understanding these interconnected entities—electrical conduction abnormalities, structural cardiac changes, specific arrhythmic syndromes, diagnostic measurements, and therapeutic interventions—is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk stratification, and management of patients with cardiac rhythm disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 4 -- Community 2783
retrieved_docs.content: Alprenolol is classified as a Class II antiarrhythmic agent; as a beta‑adrenergic blocker, it is used to manage cardiac arrhythmias by attenuating sympathetic stimulation of the heart, and its placement within the Class II antiarrhythmic category guides its therapeutic application and pharmacologic expectations.

Overall, these relationships delineate the classification, mechanisms, contraindications, and clinical relevance of tricyclic antidepressants, cyclobenzaprine, dronedarone, potassium‑sparing diuretics, aldosterone antagonists, and related agents, particularly in the management of heart failure and related cardiac disorders.

retrieved_docs.content: Paroxysmal atrial tachycardia with block is classified as a form of إِحْصار القلب غير محدد (unspecified cardiac conduction block).

A clinically important drug interaction is noted: nimodipine is contraindicated with the antiretroviral saquinavir, underscoring the need to recognize class‑specific contraindications in patient management. This integrated framework provides clinicians with a comprehensive view of structural and functional relationships, therapeutic categories, shared physiological effects, and critical interaction considerations among the listed substances.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2553
retrieved_docs.content: Antiarrhythmics, class Ic are a specific subclass within the broader category of antiarrhythmics, class I and III, indicating that they belong to the class I sodium‑channel–blocking agents rather than the class III potassium‑channel blockers. Flecainide (spelled “flekainid” in the source data) is a prototypical member of the Antiarrhythmics, class Ic group, exemplifying the pharmacologic properties that define this subclass. Understanding that class Ic antiarrhythmics are a distinct subset of class I agents and that flecainide is a representative drug helps clinicians classify antiarrhythmic therapies accurately and select appropriate treatments based on their electrophysiologic actions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2557
retrieved_docs.content: The electrophysiologic evaluation with arrhythmia induction, arrhythmogenic focus ablation, treatment of atrial fibrillation and the electrophysiologic evaluation with arrhythmia induction, arrhythmogenic focus ablation, treatment of supraventricular tachycardia are both classified as a دراسة فيزيولوجية كهربائية قلبية (cardiac electrophysiological study). Each procedure involves deliberately inducing arrhythmias, identifying the arrhythmogenic focus, performing ablation of that focus, and delivering therapeutic treatment targeted to a specific rhythm disorder—atrial fibrillation in the first case and supraventricular tachycardia in the second. As forms of cardiac electrophysiological study, they provide essential diagnostic insight and therapeutic options for managing abnormal heart rhythms.
